Field Guide Patterns that shape the system

Healthcare Laws & Paradoxes

Healthcare and health IT keep colliding with the same handful of patterns. Name them once and you start seeing them everywhere, in the metrics, the rollouts, the supply chain, the AI. One tab each.

Field Guide The Coal Question, 160 years later

Jevons Paradox

An efficiency gain inside a system doesn't reduce total consumption. It unlocks more of it. The CT scanner is the cleanest healthcare proof. The same trap is already loaded for AI.

Principle When something gets more efficient inside a system that has unmet demand, total consumption rises. Often dramatically. Efficiency is a release mechanism, not a brake.
By David Eitel ~8 min read W. Stanley Jevons, 1865
27×
US CT scan volume,
1980 → today
Same shape · 160 years apart
1865 · Britain
Coal & the steam engine.
Per-unit efficiency Improved sharplyWatt's engine, 3× efficiency
Expectation Less coal burned
What happened Total coal use ↑↑Trains, factories, ships, pumps
→ Efficiency released the system
Same loop
different fuel
2025 · Healthcare
Compute & the AI inference call.
Per-unit cost Falling fast~100× cheaper in three years
Expectation Cheaper to adopt
What's happening Total compute use ↑↑Scribes, triage, drafting, pre-reads
→ Same release valve, new fuel
Three things to remember
01
Efficiency unlocks demand. It does not reduce it.
When something gets cheaper or faster, total use of it usually goes up. Coal in 1865. CT scans after 1980. The pattern is older than computers.
02
Healthcare has unmet demand the system has been holding back.
Make any service cheaper, faster, or easier to order, and that held-back demand shows up. Often at multiples nobody planned for.
03
Cheaper AI inference is the next release valve.
Per-call cost is collapsing. The Jevons read says total compute demand goes up, not down. Whether supply keeps up is the actual question.

The economist who saw it in coal.

In 1865, William Stanley Jevons noticed something nobody wanted to hear. Steam engines had gotten dramatically more efficient. Less coal per unit of work. The expectation was obvious. Britain would burn less coal.

Britain burned more.

A lot more. Because once steam got cheap per unit, you could afford to put it everywhere. Trains. Factories. Ships. Pumps in mines that used to flood. The efficiency gain didn't shrink the system. It released the system.

"The whole of our present vast iron industry, with its multiplied uses, dates from the successful experiments of Watt." — W.S. Jevons, 1865

Watch the loop.

The mechanism is four steps. Each one is small. The combination is what does the work.

The release-valve loop
The loop closes on itself. Each round, more total use creates pressure for further efficiency, which releases more demand, which raises total use again.

CT imaging since 1980.

Early CT scanners were slow, expensive, and rare. A scan was a deliberate act. You ordered one because you really, really needed it. Roughly 3 million scans per year across the entire United States.

Then the technology got better. Helical scanning in the 90s. Multislice in the 2000s. Faster reconstruction. Lower cost per scan. Easier to read. Cheaper to own. From the outside, every change looked like efficiency.

Today the US runs more than 80 million CT scans a year. Same population. More than 25× the imaging.

CT scans per year, United States
1 dot ≈ 200,000 scans
1980
3Mscans/yr
Pre-helical CT
Today
80M+scans/yr
Post-multislice, post-cheap
Same country. Same population. Roughly 27× the imaging. Per-scan cost fell. Total spending rose.

Every individual scan is cheaper, faster, and lower-dose than 1980. The total exposure of the population to ionizing radiation went up. The total spend on imaging went up. The total downstream workup from incidental findings went up.

This is not a story about waste. Some of those 80 million scans are saving lives. The point is narrower. Efficiency did not bend the curve. It steepened it.

Counter-evidence
Not every imaging subset exploded. Bakhsheshian et al. found head CT for minor injury declined in inpatient settings between 1997 and 2014, after appropriateness criteria and clinical decision support tools were rolled in. Jevons describes a system tendency, not destiny. Where utilization gates exist and bind, the curve can flatten locally. The pattern reasserts itself wherever those gates are weak.

MRI since 1990.

Healthcare is full of these. Pick a service that got cheaper or faster, and the volume usually went somewhere it wasn't expected to. MRI ran the same play CT did.

MRI scans · United States
In 1990, the US ran about 2 million MRI scans a year. The climb since looks familiar.

The same shape, in three other places.

Once you see it, you see it everywhere. Three quick examples from outside healthcare. Same loop every time.

LED lighting
Bulbs that sip power.
Lights we run ↑ Power per bulb ↓
Cheap to run → we lit up everything.
Email
Make sending free.
Messages sent ↑ Cost per message ↓
Sending hit zero → inboxes overflowed.
Phone photos
Every photo is free to keep.
Photos you keep ↑ Cost per photo ↓
Free to keep → nothing gets deleted.

The AI version is already loaded.

Inference is getting cheaper. Cost per million tokens has fallen by orders of magnitude in three years. The standard analyst story is that this lets healthcare adopt AI at lower cost. That's the surface read.

The Jevons read is different. Cheaper inference doesn't reduce total compute use in healthcare. It unlocks new compute use. Ambient scribes for every visit. Differential diagnosis on every chief complaint. Imaging pre-reads. Discharge summary drafts. Patient messaging triage. Coding assistance. Each one is "efficient" per call. The sum is enormous.

If demand outruns infrastructure, capability doesn't just plateau. It can collapse below where it started. Workflows lock in around AI tools that aren't reliably there. Skills atrophy. Headcount that used to handle the work is gone. That isn't a science-fiction scenario. It's where the Jevons curve points if nobody is watching the supply side.

Hold this in tension
Healthcare adoption skeptics have predicted runaway AI consumption for over a decade and been wrong every time. Reimbursement rules, prior auth, clinical inertia, and integration friction have repeatedly capped utilization before compute did. Both can be true. The dam has held longer than predicted. That doesn't mean it holds forever.

The point isn't to predict collapse. It's to notice the shape of the system you're standing in. Every healthcare AI tool that gets cheaper, faster, or easier to call is pulling on the same release valve. The math from 1865 still works. It would be strange if this were the first time it didn't.

Sources
Jevons, W.S. The Coal Question. Macmillan, 1865. Original statement of the paradox.
Hong, A.S. et al. Trends in diagnostic imaging in the United States. PMID 24080717 (JAMA Internal Medicine, 2013).
Harvard Health / Collective Minds Health (2024). US imaging volume estimates.
Yale CT Validation Study, ClinicalTrials.gov ID NCT01352676.
Bakhsheshian, J. et al. Trends in head CT use after appropriateness criteria, 1997–2014. (Counter-evidence.)
OECD Health at a Glance 2025. International imaging utilization comparison.
Field GuideMetrics & incentives

Goodhart's Law

When a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure. The number keeps moving. The thing it was built to track quietly stops following it.

Principle Tie real consequences to a number and people will move the number. They will not always move the thing the number was supposed to stand for. A measure under pressure becomes a performance, not a reading.
By David Eitel ~6 min read Charles Goodhart, 1975
Three things to remember
01
A target is a measure with consequences bolted on.
The moment a number carries a bonus, a penalty, or a public ranking, effort flows to the number. Not always to the thing underneath it.
02
Healthcare runs on hundreds of proxies.
HEDIS, Star Ratings, core measures, RVUs, wait-time clocks. Each one is a stand-in for something real and hard to measure. Each one is gameable the day it starts to score.
03
The gap is the tell.
When the measure improves and the people doing the work do not believe care improved, Goodhart is running. Watch the gap, not the number.

The economist who watched a target break.

In 1975, Charles Goodhart was advising the Bank of England. Policymakers had been using monetary aggregates, the various ways of counting money in the economy, as a reliable signal of where things were heading. Then they started TARGETING those aggregates directly.

The signal fell apart almost immediately. Once the aggregate was the target, the financial system reorganized itself around hitting it, and the number stopped meaning what it used to mean. Goodhart's original 1975 phrasing was dry and technical. The version everyone quotes, "when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure," is anthropologist Marilyn Strathern's cleaner 1997 reformulation. Same law. Better sentence.

It is not a story about cheating. It is a story about what pressure does to a number. A measure at rest reflects reality. A measure under load reflects effort. Those are not the same reading, and a dashboard cannot tell them apart.

"The target was never the point. It was the finger pointing at the point. Goodhart's Law is what happens when everyone starts staring at the finger."

Watch a measure detach from reality.

A measure gets chosen because, for a while, it tracks the real thing well. That is the whole reason it earns a spot on the dashboard. Then a consequence gets attached. From that moment, the cheapest way to move the number is rarely the same as the expensive, slow way to move reality.

The two lines split. The measure keeps climbing because that is what it is now rewarded for. The thing it was supposed to represent flattens, because moving it for real was always the hard part. The space between them is the Goodhart gap, and it is pure illusion. The dashboard is green. The thing the dashboard was built to watch is not.

Healthcare is built on proxies.

Door-to-balloon time started as a genuine signal. Faster reperfusion, better outcomes in heart attack. Then it became a reported, ranked target, and the clock became something to manage. Start it a little later. Tighten the definition of when "door" counts. The reported time improves faster than the care does.

The emergency department "left without being seen" rate is supposed to measure access. Attach a target and one reliable way to improve it is to room patients quickly, then have them wait IN the room instead of the lobby. The metric clears. The wait does not.

Thirty-day readmissions are the cleanest case. Once the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program attached real Medicare penalties, the measured readmission rate fell. Observation stays and emergency-department revisits rose over the same window, absorbing patients who once would have counted as readmissions. The penalized number went down. Whether patients did better is a genuinely separate, and harder, question.

Sepsis bundle compliance, HCAHPS patient-experience scores tied to payment, RVU-driven productivity. The list is long and the pattern under it never changes. Each measure was real once. The target is what hollowed it out.

The EHR industrialized it.

Before the EHR, gaming a metric took effort. Someone had to pull charts, tally numbers, argue definitions. The EHR turned metric capture into a checkbox and metric surveillance into a live dashboard. That did not make measurement honest. It made it CHEAP. And cheap measurement breeds more measures.

Two things happen at once. Administrators add metrics, because measuring is now nearly free, and every new metric becomes a new target the moment someone decides it matters. Smart phrases, templated notes, and one-click attestations let the chart say exactly the right thing with almost no connection to what happened in the room.

The clinician is not lying. The clinician is doing precisely what a system of targets trains people to do, which is satisfy the chart. Best Practice Advisory acknowledgment rates sit near 100 percent and mean almost nothing. The advisory was acknowledged. Whether it changed a single decision is not on the dashboard.

The honest version
Not every measure rots. The ones that hold up share two traits. They are outcomes patients feel directly and cannot easily be faked around, real mortality, real function, infections that actually occurred. And the people being measured had a hand in choosing them. Goodhart bites hardest on imposed proxies. It bites least on owned outcomes.

What actually works.

You cannot run a health system without measures. The point is not to stop measuring. It is to measure in a way that resists the law instead of feeding it.

Measure closer to the outcome and further from the proxy. Pair every metric with a balancing metric, so a win cannot be faked by quietly losing somewhere else. Readmissions paired with mortality. Wait-time-to-room paired with total time in department. Rotate or retire measures before they fully harden into targets.

And above everything, WATCH THE GAP. If a measure is improving and the clinicians on the floor do not believe care is improving, believe the clinicians. The gap between the number and the felt reality is the most honest figure on the dashboard. It is also the only one nobody is trying to move.

Sources
Goodhart, C.A.E. "Problems of Monetary Management: The U.K. Experience." Papers in Monetary Economics, Reserve Bank of Australia, 1975. Original statement.
Strathern, M. "‘Improving ratings’: audit in the British University system." European Review, 1997. Source of the widely quoted phrasing.
Campbell, D.T. "Assessing the Impact of Planned Social Change." 1979. Campbell's Law, the closely related social-science formulation.
CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP), established under the Affordable Care Act, 2012. Context for the readmissions example.
CMS Hospital Value-Based Purchasing and HCAHPS. Patient-experience measures tied to payment.
Field GuideCapacity & utilization

Roemer's Law

A built bed is a filled bed. Hospital capacity, once it exists, gets used.

Principle In a system with insurance and provider-driven demand, supply creates its own utilization. Add the bed and an admission appears to fill it.

Supply is not neutral.

Milton Roemer noticed it in the 1950s. Compare two regions with similar populations and similar health. The one with more hospital beds per capita had more hospital days per capita. Not because those people were sicker. Because the beds were there, and beds get filled.

The mechanism is not mysterious. When a hospital carries empty beds, every soft admission decision tilts one way. Observe overnight instead of discharging. Admit the borderline chest pain. An empty bed is a fixed cost earning nothing, so a judgment call near the line bends toward using it.

This is Jevons wearing scrubs. It is why "just build more capacity" rarely settles a system the way the spreadsheet promised. The new MRI scanner, the new OR, the new ICU tower, they do not simply absorb existing demand. They reveal demand that was always there, waiting on the other side of a constraint.

"A bed built is a bed filled. A bed planned is a bed already promised to someone."
Field GuideTechnology & output

The Productivity Paradox

"You can see the computer age everywhere but in the productivity statistics."

Principle Robert Solow's line from 1987. Massive technology investment can land without a matching gain in measured output. Healthcare ran the experiment in full.

The EHR was supposed to pay for itself.

HITECH put roughly 27 billion dollars behind EHR adoption. The pitch was efficiency. Fewer errors, less duplication, faster care, lower cost. Adoption went from a minority of hospitals to nearly all of them in under a decade.

The productivity curve did not follow. Physicians now spend close to two hours in the EHR for every hour of direct patient care. Documentation burden is a leading, measurable driver of burnout. The technology is everywhere. The promised output gain is hard to find in the numbers.

The paradox is not that the technology does nothing. It is that the gains get spent before they ever reach the productivity line. Saved time gets absorbed by new documentation. By billing capture. By inbox volume the system could not generate before. The tool is real. The dividend went somewhere else.

Hold this in tension
Solow's paradox eventually broke. US productivity did climb in the late 1990s, once firms redesigned work around the computer instead of bolting it on. The open question for healthcare AI is whether it repeats the lag, or repeats the redesign.
Field GuideOrganizations & systems

Conway's Law

A system's design ends up copying the communication structure of the organization that built it.

Principle Melvin Conway, 1967. You ship the org chart. The seams in the software fall exactly where the seams in the organization already were.

The EHR is an org chart in software.

Walk any Epic or Oracle Health build and you can read the hospital's politics straight off the screen. Cardiology has its own order sets because cardiology has its own committee. Pharmacy and nursing do not share a workflow because pharmacy and nursing do not share a director. The system did not invent those walls. It poured concrete around the ones already standing.

This is why interoperability is so hard, and why it is not really a technical problem. Two systems do not talk because two organizations do not talk. The HL7 interface, the FHIR endpoint, the data-sharing agreement, each is a negotiation between departments wearing the costume of a negotiation between computers.

The useful read runs backward. If you want to change the software, look at the org first. A build team siloed by department will ship a siloed build every time, no matter how good the analysts are.

"You do not configure the EHR. You photograph the organization, and the EHR is the print."
Field GuideHype & time

Amara's Law

We overestimate the effect of a technology in the short run and underestimate it in the long run.

Principle Roy Amara, futurist. The hype is wrong about the date and right about the direction. Both halves of that sentence matter.

Healthcare AI is living both halves.

The short-run overestimate is loud right now. AI will read every scan by next year. The ambient scribe will end documentation burden by the next budget cycle. It will not. Integration friction, liability, reimbursement, and clinical trust all move slower than a demo.

The long-run underestimate is the quieter, more dangerous error. The same skeptics who watched IBM Watson for Oncology collapse now assume the whole category is a fad. That is the trap. Watson was the short-run overestimate. It says nothing about whether the long run is empty.

The honest position is uncomfortable. The current wave is almost certainly overhyped on TIMING, and almost certainly underhyped on EVENTUAL reach. Planning for one without the other is how organizations get caught flat. Most prepare for the version that does not arrive, and not for the version that does.

"The disappointment phase is not evidence the technology failed. It is the gap between the date you wanted and the date that was always coming."
Field GuideExpectation & maturity

The Hype Cycle

Every technology climbs a peak of inflated expectations, falls into a trough of disillusionment, then slowly finds its real level.

Principle Gartner's curve. The shape is reliable. The mistake is reading the trough as a verdict. It is a phase, not an obituary.

Five phases, one shape.

It starts with an innovation trigger, a demo, a launch, a paper. Expectations climb fast to the peak of inflated expectations, where every conference keynote promises the technology will fix everything. Then reality arrives. Pilots stall, integration is hard, and the mood drops into the trough of disillusionment. The survivors climb the slope of enlightenment as real use cases get sorted from fantasy ones, and level off at the plateau of productivity, lower than the peak, but actually real.

Healthcare keeps riding it.

Telehealth is the cleanest recent lap. It hit the peak in 2020, when every visit was suddenly virtual. By 2023 the takes had flipped to "telehealth is dead" as in-person volume returned. That was the trough. What is left now is the plateau, a smaller, durable role for the visits that genuinely work remotely.

EHRs ran the same arc over a longer decade. Meaningful Use was the peak. Burnout and "the EHR ruined medicine" was the trough. What exists now is a grudging plateau, indispensable and still resented.

The phase tells you how to act. Near the peak, the job is to resist the keynote and ask what actually integrates. In the trough, the job is the opposite, to NOT write off a technology that is simply unfashionable this year. Clinical AI is sitting near the peak right now. Plan for its trough before you are standing in it.

Hold this in tension
Not everything climbs out. Some technology dies in the trough and never reaches a plateau. IBM Watson for Oncology peaked, fell, and mostly did not return. The Hype Cycle describes a shape. It does not promise every technology completes it, and it cannot time the phases for you.
"The trough of disillusionment is where the careful people start paying attention, right as the loud people leave."
Field GuideSupply chains

The Bullwhip Effect

Small wobbles in demand at the bedside become violent swings upstream at the manufacturer.

Principle Each link in a supply chain reacts to the link in front of it, not to real demand. Every reaction amplifies the last one.

2020 was the textbook case.

A modest, real rise in demand for masks and gloves hit the front of the healthcare supply chain. Then every link did the rational thing. Hospitals, uncertain, ordered extra. Distributors saw the spike, could not separate signal from panic, and ordered more extra to protect themselves. Manufacturers saw that, and built capacity for a demand curve that was already half fear.

Then the orders that were really just hoarding got cancelled. The whip cracked back. Gluts, then shortages, then gluts again, long after bedside demand had settled. PPE was the visible one. The same wave runs quietly through saline, contrast media, and routine generic drugs every year.

The amplifier is information, not malice. Each link is blind to true demand and reacts only to the order in front of it. Health IT can dampen this, with shared demand signal instead of guarded order data, or it can make it worse, by handing every link a faster button to overreact with.

The fix is structural
The bullwhip shrinks when links share actual consumption data rather than just passing orders upstream. Visibility, not bigger buffers, is what flattens the whip. Most healthcare supply chains still run on orders.
Field GuideCompute economics

Jensen's Law

In a power-limited AI factory, spending that lifts performance per watt raises usable output faster than it raises cost.

Principle Reframed for AI infrastructure. Spend more, make more. Spend more, save more. Efficiency converts straight into throughput, because the binding constraint is power, not money.

Why this lands on healthcare.

Jensen's Law is a statement about AI compute, not about clinics. But healthcare AI does not run in a vacuum. It runs on exactly these factories. Which means the cost curve of every clinical model, every ambient scribe, every imaging pre-read, is set by whether the people building data centers can keep buying throughput faster than they buy cost.

When they can, inference keeps getting cheaper. And cheaper inference is the fuel for the Jevons loop in the first tab. The two laws are stacked. Jensen's Law sets how fast the price of a clinical AI call falls. Jevons' Paradox says that as it falls, healthcare's total demand for those calls rises to meet it.

The practical read for a health system is narrow. The economics of healthcare AI are partly out of your hands. They are being decided in power contracts and chip fabs you will never see. Plan as if the price of inference keeps falling, because the law upstream is built to make sure it does.

"Healthcare does not set the price of compute. It just lives downstream of whoever does."
A living resource for clinical informatics, healthcare data architecture, and the real story behind the systems that run modern medicine.

Built by David Eitel, RRT, MHA, MSRT, RRT-ACCS

Source Policy
All vendor-specific terminology, technical specifications, and system references on this site are derived from publicly available documentation — including fhir.epic.com, fhir.cerner.com, HL7 FHIR R4, ONC HealthIT.gov, and HHS.gov. Each factual claim is linked to its source. This site does not reproduce proprietary materials, internal vendor training content, or licensed documentation.
